Supervised Learning in R: Subscription Prediction and Model Evaluation¶

In [ ]:
library(tidyverse)
library(repr)
library(tidymodels)

Introduction¶

Questions:¶

Broad question: What player characteristics and behaviours are most predictive of subscribing to a game-related newsletter, and how do these features differ between various player types?

Specific question: Can a player's age and played hours predict whether they subscribe to a game-related newsletter in the players dataset?

We will use the players dataset, with the variables Age, played_hours, and subscribe to answer the question. Age and played_hours are our explanatory variables, and subscribe is our response variable. This specific question will help answer the first part of the broad question (What player characteristics are most predictive of subscribing to a game-related newsletter)

Why we choose these variables: We thought age and played hours would be the most telling factors for played hours, because different age groups have different preferences and spending habits, and played hours are a direct measeure of spending habits, indisputable. By using these variables, we can see gradual trends, for example "older players are more likely to subscribe". We did not use the variables gender and experience level in order to avoid overfitting by having too many factors, especially because therse variables are categorical. Also, we are not sure how the experience level is appointed, and it could cause inaccuracies if it is self-recorded. Further, gender is a personal characteristic, making it a less logical predicting factor for whether a person is subscribed.

Prediction: Players with more played hours are more likely to be subscribed, and players who are older are more likely to be subscribed.

Data Description¶

The players.csv file contains a list of 196 unique users and 7 features describing each user.
Those features include:

  • name (chr) - Player's name.

  • Age (int) - The player’s age in years, ranging from 9 to 58 years old, with median age around 19.

  • gender (chr) - The player’s gender, seven categories of the gender of the player, 'Male', 'Female', 'Non-binary', 'Prefer not to say', 'Agender', 'Two-Spirited', 'Other'

  • experience (chr) - Indicates a player's experience level. Possible levels include:
    Beginner, Amateur, Regular, Pro, and Veteran.

  • played_hours (dbl) - Total hours the player has played. Hours range from 0 to 223.1, with a mean of 5.85 and a median of 0.1.

  • subscribe (lgl) - Indicates whether the player subscribed to a game-related newsletter (TRUE or FALSE). Out of 196 players, 144 (≈73.5%) subscribed, and 52 (≈26.5%) did not.

  • hashedEmail (chr) - Player’s email stored as a hashed value for privacy.

Methods and Plan:¶

Method: We will use KNN classification to try to predict whether or not a player subscribes to the game-related newsletter using two explanatory variables: Age and played_hours. Since the response variable (subscribe) is categorical (TRUE/FALSE), using classification is appropriate, and since our prediction methods are numerical, using KNN is appropriate

Assumptions: We are assuming that empty rows are removed from the dataset, as well as that neither predictor is contributing more than the other (I can make sure of this by scaling the data)

Limitations: Some limitations of using KNN classification are that if there is no relationship between the variables, KNN will still provide answers, but they may not be accurate. Since our graphs show weak relationships, this could be an issue, so we should try making some different types of graphs to see a relationship.

Process:

1. Standardize data so that `Age` and `played_hours` are contributing equally
2. Split data into training (75%) and testing (25%) sets
3. Use cross-validation on the training set to find the best k (choose the k that gives the most accuracy)
4. Train a KNN model on the training set, then use it on the testing set

Exploratory Data Analysis:¶

Numerical variable Average value
played_hours 5.85
Age 21.14
In [2]:
library(readr)
players_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/chantalfl18/project_template/refs/heads/main/Project/players.csv'
players <- read_csv(players_url)

#finding summary statistics
avg_played_hours <- players |>
    summarise(mean = mean(played_hours, na.rm = TRUE))

avg_age <- players |>
    summarise(mean = mean(Age, na.rm = TRUE))
avg_played_hours
avg_age

#Figure 1

options(repr.plot.width = 13, repr.plot.height = 8)
hours_vs_age_plot <- ggplot(players, aes(x=Age, y=played_hours, color=subscribe)) +
       geom_point() +
       labs(title = "Hours of game played vs Age", x="Age", y="Hours of game played", color = "Subscribed to game-related newsletter") +
    theme(text=element_text(size= 20))
hours_vs_age_plot

    labs(title = "Age Distribution by Subscription Status", x= "Age", y = "Count") +
    theme(text=element_text(size= 20))

In [14]:
#Figure 3

hours_bar_plot <- ggplot(players, aes(x = played_hours, fill = subscribe)) +
    geom_histogram(position = "identity", alpha = 0.5, bins = 10) +
    labs(title = "Played hours Distribution by Subscription Status", x= "Hours of game played", y = "Count") +
    theme(text=element_text(size= 20))
hours_bar_plot

Fig. 3. Histogram plot that shows subscriber count across the number of played hours. The lowest played-hours group contains only FALSE subscribers, suggesting a possible negative relationship, but the evidence is limited and not strong enough for a conclusion.

Data Analysis¶

In [6]:
# Cleaning the data, removing NAs 
players_clean <- players |>
  filter(!is.na(Age)) |> filter(!is.na(played_hours)) |> filter(!is.na(subscribe))
In [7]:
# Mutate Subscribe as a factor
players_clean <- players_clean |>
  mutate(subscribe = as.factor(subscribe))
In [8]:
# Creating training and test sets
set.seed(123)
players_split <- initial_split(players_clean, prop = 0.75, strata = subscribe)
players_train <- training(players_split)
players_test <- testing(players_split)
In [9]:
# Create recipe with Age and played_hours as the predictor and subscribe as the response variable
players_recipe <- recipe(subscribe ~ Age + played_hours, data = players_train) |> step_normalize(all_predictors())
In [10]:
# Create model & workflow
players_spec <- nearest_neighbor(mode = "classification", neighbors = tune(),         
  weight_func = "rectangular") |>
  set_engine("kknn")

players_workflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(players_spec) |>
  add_recipe(players_recipe)
In [11]:
set.seed(123)
# Model tuning
folds <- vfold_cv(players_train, v = 5, strata = subscribe)

players_grid <- tibble(neighbors = seq(1, 51, by = 2))

players_tuned <- tune_grid(players_workflow, resamples = folds, grid = players_grid, metrics = metric_set(accuracy))

results <- collect_metrics(players_tuned)

select_best_k <- results |>
  filter(.metric == "accuracy") |>
  arrange(desc(mean))
    
#Figure 4

select_best_k |>
  ggplot(aes(x = neighbors, y = mean)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Cross-validated Accuracy for Different K Values",
       x = "Number of Neighbors (k)",
       y = "Mean Accuracy") + theme(text = element_text(size = 14))

# Therefore, we notice that the best K value is 9, so we will store it. 
best_k <- 9

Fig. 4. Line graph that shows the cross-validated accuracy for different K values, with the number of neighbours on the X axis and the mean accuracy on the y axis. There is a noticeable elbow at $K = 9$, so we will choose this as our K value

In [12]:
# Rebuild with the best K 
players_model_bestk <- nearest_neighbor (mode = "classification",neighbors = best_k) |> set_engine("kknn")
players_workflow_bestk <- workflow() |>
  add_model(players_model_bestk) |>
  add_recipe(players_recipe)


players_fit_bestk <- fit(players_workflow_bestk, data = players_train)
# Predict & generate Accuracy and Confusion matrix
prediction <- predict(players_fit_bestk, players_test, type = "class") |> bind_cols(players_test |> select(subscribe))
metrics(prediction, truth = subscribe, estimate = .pred_class)
conf_mat(prediction, truth = subscribe, estimate = .pred_class)

prediction_1 <- prediction |> bind_cols(players_test |> select(Age, played_hours)) |> mutate(correct = (.pred_class == subscribe))
A tibble: 2 × 3
.metric.estimator.estimate
<chr><chr><dbl>
accuracybinary0.6530612
kap binary0.1711443
 
          Truth
Prediction FALSE TRUE
     FALSE     6   10
     TRUE      7   26
In [13]:
# Created scatterplot graph of KNN Classification results on the Test Set
#Figure 5
players_knn_scatter <- prediction_1 |> ggplot(aes(x = Age, y = played_hours)) + geom_point(aes(color = .pred_class, shape = correct), size = 3, alpha = 0.6) + labs(title = "KNN Classification Results on the Test Set", x = "Age (years)", y = "Played Hours", color = "Predicted Subscribe", shape = "Prediction Correct?") +  theme(text = element_text(size = 14))
players_knn_scatter

Fig. 5. Scatterplot graph that shows us the KNN classification results on the testing set, with Age on the X axis and played hours on the Y axis. The observations that were predicted to be subscribed are in blue, while those that were expected not to be subscribed are in red. These predictions are also separated into those that were true (triangles) and those that were false (circles).

Discussion:¶

After analysis of the dataset, our prediction model was determined to be 65% accurate for predicting subscription status. Because the response variable is binary, this level of accuracy is only slightly better than random guessing (50%) and therefore indicates that the model does not perform particularly well.

From analyzing Figure 2, we can see that the majority of subscribers are around the age of 15-25. In the plot in Figure 3, we can infer that the majority of players with 25+ hours are subscribed, with only players in the lowest played hours group containing non-subscribers. Our findings supported our initial prediction that more played hours would result in a higher subscription rate. The age predictor, however, did not support the initial prediction that the different spending habits of different ages would result in different subscription rates. Due to the majority of players being aged 15-30, age is not a strong predictor of subscription status. These findings can allow the game creators to further cater to an audience that does not subscribe, increasing potential profits. For example, the game developers can target more players aged in the 20-30 range to further increase the percentage of subscribers. Despite these findings, future research can be done to determine if other factors, such as experience, impact subscription rate or if they only impact the other predictors. Understanding these things would allow us to create a more accurate prediction model that developers could use to further develop features that encourage players to subscribe.
